story:
Wizard Owl had watched over the forest for centuries, as patient and quiet as the wind in the leaves. His big brown eyes missed very little, and his speckled white feathers made him look as old and wise as the trees themselves. The animals came to him for help—“Please, wise old Mr. Owl, make it rain!” “Please, wise old Mr. Owl, bring back the sunshine!”—and he always tried to keep everyone happy.

On a warm spring morning, dew shining on the grass and sunlight slipping through a green canopy, the forest woke to a special day: Grandfather Hare’s one hundred and eleventh birthday. Wizard Owl had arranged the perfect weather—clear skies, a gentle breeze—and the creatures were busy with preparations. The squirrels were baking a cake inside the big oak, the birds were stringing bright streamers in the clearing, and frogs were rehearsing a musical number for the old hare.

As Wizard Owl glided overhead, pleased to see everything going according to plan, he caught the faint scent of smoke. He swooped to the oak tree, where he found squirrels racing in a panic and smoke curling from the entrance. “What happened?” he asked, swiveling his head almost all the way around to take in the scene. With the flames rising fast, he flapped his wings and summoned clouds. Cool raindrops thickened into a steady downpour until the fire hissed and died. The squirrels cheered, but their birthday cake was spoiled. Wizard Owl soothed them; the party would go on.

He cleared the storm and restored the soft spring sunshine—only to find the birds drooping beneath sagging, wet streamers. His rain had soaked the decorations. “Don’t worry,” he said. He conjured the driest summer heat he could muster to save the streamers. Little by little they dried, and the birds cheered—until they tried to straighten the crinkled ribbons, only for them to crumble in their beaks. There was no fixing them.

“It’s still going to be the best birthday yet,” Wizard Owl promised. “We have the music!” But the frogs came forward looking glum. The sudden heat had parched their throats; even the frogs’ croaks sounded dusty. Trying to undo the damage, Wizard Owl stirred a light rain to ease their voices.

Just then Grandfather Hare appeared through the brush, waistcoat neat, walking stick tapping. “Good morning, all!” he said as fat raindrops began to speckle his ears. Wizard Owl, startled, flapped to stop the shower, but his tired wings only thickened the clouds. The gentle sprinkle became a real downpour.

“It’s Grandfather Hare! Happy birthday!” everyone cried.

“Follow me!” the old hare called. He led the squirrels, birds, frogs, and Wizard Owl down into his den. It was crowded but warm, and he brewed tea for everyone. Wizard Owl drooped his feathers. “I wanted to make your one hundred and eleventh the best ever,” he said. “But every time I tried to fix one thing, I made something else worse.”

“Come now, wise old owl,” Grandfather Hare chuckled. “A birthday is not the sky above it, but the company around it.” And so they held a merry tea party underground while the rain pattered above. Grandfather Hare had a wonderful birthday after all, and Wizard Owl learned to use his weather magic with more care—so that kindness, like sunshine and rain, would fall in the right measure.
